Q1: I tried really hard to keep a consistent study routine, usually dedicating about 3-4 hours each day across the week. I'm a hands-on learner, so I would often work through problems physically, writing things out multiple times. But honestly, even with all those hours, I felt like I was spinning my wheels a lot. My anxiety made it hard to focus sometimes, so I'd sit there for hours but not absorb as much as I should have.
Q2: I tried to keep regular times, mostly evenings after work since I'm 27 and have other responsibilities to manage. But when deadlines approached, everything fell apart and I'd end up studying late into the night. The stress would build up and my pattern would become chaotic. I wish I could say I was more disciplined, but life kept getting in the way.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new assignments posted or if grades had come back. I logged in pretty frequently, almost daily, because I was always worried I might miss something important. The anxiety of potentially falling behind kept me checking constantly. Sometimes I'd log in just to reassure myself that I hadn't missed a deadline.
Q4: Yes, checking the LMS became almost compulsive for me. Every morning with my tea, I'd open it up and scan through announcements and upcoming tasks. It became part of my daily routine, though I'm not sure it was always healthy because sometimes I'd just stare at the dashboard feeling overwhelmed. The habit formed out of fear more than anything else.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful to me because I could pause and rewind when concepts didn't click right away. I watched almost 290 minutes of video content, often replaying sections multiple times. As a kinesthetic learner, I'd take notes while watching and try to work through examples alongside the instructor. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when other students explained things in different ways.
Q6: I mostly ignored the optional reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. With 17 courses to manage, I just couldn't get to everything, so I prioritized what was directly assessed. Some resources felt too theoretical without enough practical application, which doesn't work well for how I learn. I always meant to go back to them but never found the time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately apply what I learned or when it directly connected to an assignment. I needed to see the practical purpose, otherwise my motivation just wasn't there. If I could work through something with my hands, like solving problems or creating something, that felt valuable. Abstract discussions without clear outcomes drained me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With my anxiety already high and so many required tasks, optional work just added to my mental load without the urgency to push me through. Required activities had deadlines that forced me to act, even when I didn't feel capable. The optional ones would sit there making me feel guilty for not doing them.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then print them out so I could highlight and annotate by hand. I also used a physical notebook constantly, rewriting concepts and working through problems. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos that explained things better than our course materials.
Q10: There were many times I'd be at my kitchen table with printed materials spread everywhere, working through problems for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd access a resource once to download it, then spend days studying from that printout. My best learning happened away from the screen, with a pen in my hand. The system couldn't capture any of that effort.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach actually. I'd download everything at the start of each module and then work from those files offline. My internet isn't always reliable, so having offline copies was essential. I'd annotate the PDFs, print key sections, and return to them repeatedly over days or weeks. One download could represent hours of subsequent study.
Q12: Around mid-semester, everything became harder. My exam scores weren't reflecting my effort, and I started feeling hopeless about whether any of this work mattered. The sadness just crept in and made even logging into the LMS feel exhausting. I went through a CBT session which helped a bit, but the low mood persisted and made engagement feel like pushing through mud.
Q13: The workload across 17 courses was genuinely overwhelming, and my medium stress level often spiked to unbearable. When I was exhausted, I'd log into the LMS but just scroll mindlessly without really engaging. Competing responsibilities as a 27-year-old meant I couldn't always prioritize coursework the way younger students might. Confusion about concepts would paralyze me sometimes, making me avoid materials I didn't understand.
Q14: Many times, honestly. I'd have videos playing while my mind wandered to everything I was worried about. I'd read the same paragraph multiple times without it sinking in because my anxiety was too loud. The LMS would register that I watched something or opened a document, but my brain wasn't really there. It's frustrating because the time was spent but not productively.
Q15: I avoided the exam preparation materials until the last possible moment because they triggered my anxiety. I knew they were important, but facing them meant confronting how much I didn't understand. The assignments I could handle because they were broken into steps, but open-ended exam study felt paralyzing. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that could cut through my low motivation and force action. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find energy I didn't have before. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. Without deadlines, I'd drift and struggle to prioritize anything, so the structure was actually helpful for someone like me.
Q17: Feedback affected me deeply, sometimes too deeply. When I got lower scores than expected, it would send me into a spiral of self-doubt that made it harder to engage with subsequent materials. Positive feedback gave me brief motivation boosts, but they'd fade quickly. I started dreading checking my grades because the disappointment was hard to recover from emotionally.
Q18: The weekly module structure helped me stay somewhat organized, giving me clear checkpoints to work toward. But with so many courses running simultaneously, the structures overlapped and created chaos. I appreciated when things were clearly laid out, but the volume was just too much. The structure was good in theory but overwhelming in practice.
Q19: Having practical, hands-on activities made engagement much easier for me as a kinesthetic learner. When modules were purely reading-based without application, I struggled to stay connected. The discussion requirements helped force some interaction, but with only 5 posts, you can see I didn't go beyond the minimum. Clearer connections between concepts and real-world applications would have helped.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat, actually. I was highly active in the LMS, logging in frequently and watching lots of videos, but my assignment scores and exam performance didn't match that activity. Sometimes you're going through the motions without truly understanding. High anxiety can make you check things obsessively without actually learning. The activity is there, but the deep processing isn't.
Q21: I can see how that happens, though it wasn't my experience. Some people are efficient learners who don't need to spend as much time in the system. They might have better focus, less anxiety, or stronger background knowledge. For me, my high activity reflected my struggle, not my success. I needed more time and repetition, but it still wasn't enough.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, time spent, and completion of tasks, which gives some picture of effort. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and what's happening in someone's head. They can't capture offline study, emotional state, or whether someone actually understood anything. My logs would show dedication, but my grades show I was struggling despite all that visible effort.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, not just going through motions. It's when concepts click and you can apply them, when you're curious rather than just compliant. For me, true engagement happened in those quiet moments with my notebook, working through problems until they made sense. It's about connection with the content, not just time logged in a system.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I cried from frustration or how my anxiety made simple tasks feel impossible. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials at my kitchen table or the YouTube tutorials I found helpful. It can't show that I genuinely cared and tried hard, even though I failed. My emotional journey, my offline efforts, my struggles with mental health, none of that appears in the data.
